instruction: Head across the room towards the front door then turn left. Go up the stairs and turn right. Enter the bedroom then wait.

text: I'm in the living room with a fireplace and need to navigate to the bedroom—heading toward the front door, turning left up the stairs, then right into the bedroom. Let me look around for the front door.
I'm at the starting position in the living room with a fireplace and chairs marked as a landmark. Now I'll scan to locate the front door direction.

I'm mapping out the spatial layout of this room from the current viewpoint. Looking ahead is the living room with a fireplace and clear sightlines. To the right is the dining area with a table and a doorway leading to what looks like a bathroom. Behind me is a built-in cabinet or butler's pantry, and to the left is another seating area with a sofa and what appears to be a glass-paneled front door visible in the distance.

The front door seems to be the white paneled door with windows on the far left side of the room. I should head left and move across the room to get closer to it and confirm it's the entrance.
I've identified a white glass-paneled door at 270° that appears to be the front door. Now I'll turn left and head toward it.

I've hit a wall at the 1.0m mark near some furniture—looks like a sofa and lamp in the sitting area. There's a glass-paneled cabinet or door on the left, a window ahead, and I'm trying to figure out where the front entrance is, which should be somewhere near the foyer.
I'm blocked near the sofa corner with a glass-paneled door to my left. Now I'll scan for the foyer and front door.

I'm visualizing the left view of the foyer with the front door on the right and staircase on the left, which matches the navigation path. I need to turn left 90 degrees and walk forward about 2 meters to reach the foyer, then ascend the stairs, turn right, and enter the bedroom. The execution plan is to make six left turns, walk forward roughly 8 steps (about 2 meters), and observe the result.
I've identified the foyer with the front door ahead-right and stairs on the left. Next I'll turn left and walk about 2.5 meters to reach the base of the stairs.

I'm orienting myself in the foyer at the console table with the mirror. The front door is to my right and the stairs are behind me on the left—I can see the stair railing at the far left edge of the frame. This matches the instruction to turn left at the front door toward the stairs.
At the foyer console table (mirror). Stairs should be to my left. EXPECT: after turning left 90°, staircase base ahead.

I'm looking at the staircase layout ahead of me — it rises from the left side of the frame upward to the right with a landing that turns. The base is positioned slightly left of center, and there's about 0.8 meters of clearance in the middle where the newel or plant obstruction is. I need to adjust my position by turning left roughly 45 degrees to navigate around the bottom of the stairs.
Staircase ahead, ascending from the left. EXPECT: after turning left ~90°, the bottom steps are directly ahead.

I'm trying to track my spatial orientation as I turn—I rotated 90° left from facing the staircase, which should put the stairs roughly 45° to my right now, but the view shows the living room with what looks like stair railing visible at the right edge, so I may have overturned or misjudged the angle.

Let me rotate right 45° to reorient myself and get a clearer view of where the stairs actually are.
✗ Over-rotated — facing the living room again. Stair base should be ~45° right. Correcting.
I'm orienting myself to the staircase on my right—the steps ascend toward the upper right, so I need to turn right about 60 to 90 degrees and move forward to start climbing.
Stairs on my right. Turning right to face up the staircase, then climbing. EXPECT: view rises up the stairs toward a landing.
I've moved to the plant beside the staircase and can see the steps ascending to my left. I need to turn about 90 degrees to face the stairs and attempt to climb them.
✗ Blocked at the plant beside the stairs; steps visible on my left. Turning left to face up the stairs and climbing.
I'm on the lower steps of the staircase, looking across them with the living room visible below to my left. I need to turn right about 90 degrees to face up the stairs.
On the lower steps; staircase ascends to my right. Turning right and climbing. EXPECT: reach the landing/upper floor.

I'm trying to figure out my position in the foyer — I'm stuck near the plant at the base of the stairs, sliding off when I try to climb. Looking back at the earlier view, the stairs are on the left side of the foyer, ascending from the lower-right toward the upper-left, with the front door on the right side of the frame.

The issue is that I keep colliding with the plant at the outer corner of the staircase. When I was on the lower steps and turned right to ascend, I slid into it. I should back away from the plant first and use look_around to get a clearer sense of where I am before attempting the stairs again.
Stuck at the plant again — sliding off the stair edge. Backing off and re-orienting with a full scan.

Looking at the spatial layout more carefully — the ahead view shows an upstairs hallway with a stairwell descending through the middle, while the right view appears to show the front door at ground level, which is creating some confusion about the actual floor I'm on.

Let me reconsider the geometry: if the foyer is double-height (as seen earlier), I could be viewing the front door from an upper landing or balcony overlooking it. The behind view showing the staircase with the living room beyond suggests I'm positioned somewhere along the stair flight itself, possibly mid-level or at ground floor beside the main stairwell. The railing pattern in the ahead view — surrounding a downward stair opening — could indicate I'm actually on the ground floor next to a basement stairwell rather than upstairs, which would explain why the front door appears at roughly eye level in the right view.

So the layout is becoming clearer: I'm in the entry hallway near the front door, with the main ascending staircase behind me (visible in the behind view rising from left to right), a basement stair ahead going downward, and the living room beyond the main stair. To climb the main staircase, I'd need to move toward the foyer area near the front door, turn to face away from it, and then ascend the first flight. So from the front door, turning left faces the staircase. I'm working out the geometry: the stairs occupy the left side of the foyer when approaching from the living room, ascending toward the hallway with a landing partway up. Rather than getting caught in bearing calculations, I should just position myself near the door facing the console table wall, then move left toward the stair base and climb along its run.

Looking at the views again to confirm my position: the ahead view shows a wood hallway with descending stairs on the left (those must be basement stairs) and doors ahead, while the right view shows the front door at eye level with the floor mat visible—no elevated railing, so I'm definitely downstairs. The behind view shows the main staircase from the side, with the bottom step near the plant on the ground, and the flight ascending toward my position. The stair base is toward the living room side, and I need to figure out the exact bearing to approach it correctly.

I think the best move is to physically navigate into the foyer—turn right 90 degrees and move forward a few steps—then look around from there to get a clearer sense of the layout.
I'm in the hallway by the stairs and can see the front door ahead. I'll move to the foyer by the front door first, then head to the stair base.

I'm visualizing the foyer layout with the front door ahead and a console table to the left. To the right, the staircase rises with its bottom steps curving toward the foyer, while the living room extends beyond. Behind me, another section of the staircase ascends to the upper left.

Now I'm working through the spatial geometry—the stairs run along a wall, and from the front door facing into the house, they climb up and to the left. The instruction to turn left and go up the stairs means I need to rotate 180° to face away from the door, then navigate toward the staircase base.

My plan: make 12 left turns to achieve the 180° rotation, move forward 1-2 steps to reach the stairs, turn left another 45°, then climb forward 8-10 steps while observing the geometry.
I'm at the front door and can see the staircase in the view ahead. I'll turn left and climb the stairs toward the landing.
I'm at the base of the stairs now, with the wall about 0.4 meters to my right. The staircase ascends toward the upper-left, so I need to turn left about 30-45 degrees and move forward up the steps.
At the stair base; flight ascends up-left of my heading. Aligning left ~45° and climbing hard.
I'm climbing the stairs now with a view overlooking the living room—the banister runs along my left side while the wooden steps continue ascending to my right. I need to turn right and keep going up.
On the staircase now — elevated view over the living room. Flight continues up to my right. Turning right 90° and climbing.

I keep getting funneled back to that corner plant by the staircase—each time I climb and turn right, something about the geometry or the banister seems to slide me back down to the ground floor. Let me check if this is actually the same ground floor view as before by comparing it to an earlier frame.

Looking at the staircase geometry more carefully: it's clearly a winding or L-shaped layout. When I was midway up at t27 facing the living room over the railing, I thought the steps to my right were ascending, but turning right and moving forward actually took me back down toward the plant corner—so those steps were descending, not ascending. The actual ascent must have been to my left, but the banister was right there blocking that path. I'm getting confused about which direction is actually uphill in this curved staircase.

From the original foyer view, the staircase appears to be an L or U shape: bottom steps at the center, flight ascending toward the left edge, then a switchback landing, and an upper flight at the top-left. The behind view from t23 confirms the flight rises from bottom-right to top-left with the landing at the top-left. So at t25 when I was at the base facing up, I need to figure out which direction actually leads upward.

My mistake was turning right at the midpoint—I should have turned left instead to continue up the switchback. Let me trace back through the successful movements: from the plant corner, I previously got onto the lower steps by turning left 90° and moving forward, then from there turning right led me back into the plant. So the staircase base is toward the foyer side, and I need to reconstruct the exact sequence of turns that actually got me climbing.

Looking at my current position facing 180° at the plant corner, the steps appear at the left edge of my view, which means they're bearing around 225°. I'll turn left to face that direction and move forward a few steps to get a clearer view of the stair base.
I realized I was facing the wrong direction at t27 — the slope was on my left, not right. I'm re-climbing now, facing 225° and keeping left at the midpoint.

I've confirmed this matches the target frame — at the stair base with the flight ascending slightly left and a wall on the right. Now I'm adjusting my movement sequence: I'll move forward with a shorter vertical component this time, then turn left more aggressively and continue climbing with an even tighter angle, batching the movements together to test this approach before observing the result.
I've confirmed the anchor point at the stair base and traced a climbing path with left turns. Now I'll proceed to the upstairs landing.

I've slid off the stairs again and ended up back at the ground floor near the front entrance, which suggests the navigation mesh on those winding steps is too narrow or my approach angle is off. Looking at the clearest scan data, I need to reconsider the stair geometry more carefully—the deflection happening at the bottom steps indicates the path might require a more precise heading to navigate successfully. I'm realizing that glass-paneled door at the sitting area corner is likely the den entrance, not the front door—the actual front entrance is the white double door with sidelights I saw earlier. So I'm positioned near that den door with the stair base somewhere behind me toward the foyer. Rather than navigate the curved bottom steps, I should try walking straight up along the flight's wall side, but I need to figure out which direction the flight actually runs—the t27 frame showed me partway up with the banister diagonal across the view, living room below-left, and steps descending to the right, suggesting the uphill direction was roughly toward bearing 315-340°.

Looking back at the clearance data from t27 (L0.2 C0.2 R1.2), the right side was most open, which contradicts the idea that I should climb leftward. When I turned right and moved forward, I ended up at the plant corner at ground level, which makes me wonder if I was actually on the landing above the plant corner at t27, and my forward movement descended the first flight rather than continuing up. If that's the case, the second flight would've been to my left at t27, where the 0.2 clearance reading suggests either a wall or railing blocking that direction.

My global coordinate tracking has gotten too muddled from the sliding movements, so I'm switching to a simpler approach: I'll hug the wall as I climb, moving 2-3 steps at a time and checking my heading between advances to stay aligned with the upstairs direction. This should take about 5-6 observations and I have 47 calls remaining, so it's feasible. Right now I'm near the front door facing the sofa, and the stair base is off to my right toward the foyer, so I'll start by looking around to reorient myself.
The robot slid off the steps again near the front door, so I'm switching to an incremental climbing approach with re-anchoring and visual correction between each set of steps.

I'm orienting myself in the foyer now. Behind me is the staircase—a full view with the flight ascending from the lower area up toward the top-left, landing at an upper balcony with railings visible at the top-center. There are plants and vases positioned at a mid-frame corner, and the bottom steps are directly ahead to my left, roughly 1-2 meters away at a bearing around 200-210 degrees. To my left is the front door.

To my right, I can see the staircase from another angle—the curved bottom steps visible at the right edge of the frame. The stair base sits somewhere between the right view and the behind view, so the bottom steps are positioned between bearings 90 and 180 degrees. Looking at the behind view, the flight ascends toward the upper-left, which means the climbing direction heads roughly toward bearing 225 degrees.

I'm planning to turn around to face the staircase directly, then move forward to the base of the steps before climbing. I'll execute a series of turns to reorient myself, then step forward twice to reach the stairs, and observe from there.
I've identified the staircase directly behind me about 1.5 meters away. I'll turn around and start climbing.

I'm orienting myself in the hallway with the staircase immediately to my left, its banister and steps taking up that space as it ascends from behind me toward the landing ahead. The flight runs roughly parallel to my current direction, climbing from the foyer upward with the plant and vases positioned at the corner ahead-left.

The bottom step is actually back in the foyer where I started, about 1.5 to 2 meters behind me. I can't mount the stairs from here by turning left slightly—I'd just hit the side rail. I need to backtrack to the foyer first, reorient myself at the staircase entry, then approach the flight properly from its base.

Let me try a clearer sequence: turn 180 degrees to face the foyer, walk forward about 3 meters to get back there, then reposition myself at the actual bottom step. The winding stairs curve up from the foyer facing the front door, so I need to mount them heading away from the front door—which is roughly my current bearing but from 2 meters back and 1 meter to the left. Actually, I remember getting partway up successfully before (around the midway point in an earlier scan). Let me just retrace that path: get back to the base frame view where the steps are ahead of me, the wall is on my right, and the window is visible behind-left, then repeat the moves that worked.
I see the flight is to my left going up, with the entry behind me in the foyer. I'm heading back a couple steps and repositioning to face up the stairs.

I'm trying to map out the foyer layout and getting turned around on the stair positioning. I walked forward from the hallway and passed the stair base, so I'm now at the sitting room edge facing away from where I came. The stairs seem to mount in the opposite direction from the sitting area, which means I'd need to turn back around to access them. Let me establish a fixed reference point—the foyer is a square with the front door on the north wall and the console table with mirror on the west side.

From my earlier scan, the stairs run along the east side ascending toward the south, with the living room in the southwest and the sitting area where I am now. When I turned to face the stairs and walked forward, I apparently drifted into the hallway entrance instead of hitting the flight directly—the stairs were slightly to my left. So on my next attempt, I need to aim left of my previous heading to actually reach the flight rather than the hallway again.

Rather than keep calculating bearing offsets, I'll just turn around in 90-degree increments until I'm facing the stair base directly, then observe as I climb step by step. I have plenty of calls left, so I'll take it methodically—turning about 135 degrees right from here to face the stairs, then observing before I start ascending.
Yo-yoing around the stair base. Turning ~135° right to face it visually before mounting.

I've got a clear view looking straight up the staircase—it ascends directly ahead with a slight rightward curve toward the top landing, and I can see gold plates on the wall marking the alignment. The stairs veer right as they go up, so I'll need to move forward and adjust my direction rightward as I climb. Let me advance five steps and reassess from there.
I've confirmed the flight curves right near the top. Now I'll climb in short increments to observe the pattern.

I'm climbing up the stairs now, several steps in. The staircase curves to the right ahead, so I need to adjust my footing as I plant on the right side through the turn, then continue forward with a slight rightward shift for about four more steps.
✓ On the flight, climbing. Steps wind right ahead. Adjusting right 30°, 4 forward.

I'm halfway up the staircase now, turning left at about thirty degrees and moving forward about four steps. The upper flight stretches ahead and to the left toward the upstairs hallway where I can see pictures on the wall, with the banister running along my right side.
✓ At the winder turn; upper flight continues up-left. Left 30°, 4 forward.
I'm at the gold leaf plates where the wall turns, and the staircase continues down to the right. I need to pivot about 45 to 60 degrees and keep climbing.
Facing the wall at the turn; steps continue to my right. Right 45°, 4 forward.

Almost there — I can see the upstairs hallway ahead with the wooden floor and wall art, and just need to climb the last few steps to reach the upper landing, then turn right and walk forward a bit to get to the top.
✓ Top of stairs in sight — upstairs hall ahead-right. Right 30°, 5 forward to top out.

I've reached the top of the stairs and can see a bedroom to my left with a bed and window, but the instruction says to turn right. I'm trying to figure out my current orientation after climbing the winding stairs to determine which direction is actually right.
✓ Topped out on the upper landing — a bedroom is visible ahead-left. Instruction says turn right at the top; scanning to find the correct bedroom.

I'm mapping out the upstairs layout from the scan — there's a banister rail ahead with a bedroom doorway to the left, a hallway extending right with a mirror console and a bright room at the end, the stairwell void behind me where I came up, and closed white double doors on the left that look like a closet. I was heading roughly north when I climbed the stairs.

Now I'm trying to figure out which bedroom the instruction refers to — the one I can see ahead-left with the green bed cover, or the larger bright room down the hallway to the right with the chandelier. The stairs wound rightward at the top, so when I emerged, I was probably facing toward the white doors, which means "turn right" would point me toward the visible bedroom ahead. But the hallway to the right also opens into what looks like a bedroom entrance with a chandelier, about 4.5 meters away. The instruction "go up the stairs and turn right, enter the bedroom" is ambiguous about which one it means. turn right from the final flight would lead me toward the hallway with the chandelier room, but the bedroom with the green blanket is immediately to my left and clearly visible. I'm weighing whether "turn right, enter the bedroom" means the closer bedroom I can see now, or if the chandelier room at the end of the hallway is the intended destination—the mirror and console I saw on the upper landing seem to connect to that direction. Turning right would head toward the hallway with the chandelier room (B), while turning left leads to bedroom A. Since I'm uncertain whether room B is actually a bedroom or something else like a bathroom, I'll explore down the hallway to check it out—if it's a bedroom I'll enter, otherwise I'll backtrack to bedroom A. I'll turn right and move forward about 2 meters along the hall to get a better look.
I'm at the upstairs landing with a bedroom visible ahead-left and a bright room with a chandelier to the right. I'll head right down the hallway to check if that bright room is also a bedroom.

So Room B is the master bedroom at the end of the hallway with the chandelier—I can see the big bed with white and blue bedding, the ceiling light, and french doors opening to the balcony. The directions match up: going up the stairs and turning right leads directly into this room. There's some kind of mesh artifact or railing glitch blocking the view at about 0.6 meters in front, though the thought cuts off there.

I need to navigate through the doorway ahead—it's slightly to the left of center. I'll angle left about 15 degrees and move forward roughly 3 steps to center myself in the doorway, then continue forward 4-6 steps to fully enter the bedroom and get a clearer view.
✓ Room B IS a bedroom (master — bed, french doors). Entering: nudge left, push through the doorway.

I'm navigating through a hallway toward a bedroom doorway that's slightly to the right, with about 2.3 meters of clearance. There's some kind of dark mesh object—possibly a bench or luggage rack—blocking part of the floor, so I'm moving cautiously forward. The bed is visible through the doorway ahead, and I need to continue advancing about 4-5 meters to pass through it.
Doorway dead ahead, 2.3 m clear through it. Pushing inside.
The clearance values haven't changed after running six forwards, which means the dark mesh artifact is blocking the progress—I need to observe this to confirm what's happening.

There's a dark mesh obstruction blocking the direct path through the middle of the hall. I can navigate around it by taking the left side where there's about 1.3 meters of clearance, then execute a simple detour: angle left 45 degrees, move forward 3 units, turn right 45 degrees, and continue forward 4 units to get past the obstacle.
[BLOCKED] by a mesh artifact spanning the hall. Detouring around its left side.
I've made it to the bedroom doorway—the door frame is on my left and the bed is visible inside to the right. A couple more steps forward and a right turn should get me fully into the room.
✓ At the bedroom threshold. Stepping inside: right 30°, 4 forward.

text: I'm now inside the bedroom, having made it through the sequence of movements—across the room, to the front door, up the stairs, and into this space. I can see the bed on my left and the dresser with mirror on the right, with french doors ahead. I'm just a step or two past the threshold, so I'll take one more step to ensure I'm fully inside, then stop as instructed.
I've navigated through the bedroom and completed all the movement instructions. Now I'll take one more step forward and stop.

text: I'm checking that the endpoint placement is correct—ending just inside the bedroom after entering matches the instruction "Enter the bedroom then wait." Since I'm positioned a couple steps in, that's the right spot to stop.
The final clause "Enter the bedroom then wait" places the endpoint just inside the bedroom where I'm standing, so I'm confirming this is the correct stopping point.

text:
Episode complete — STOP executed inside the bedroom.

**Final ledger:**
```
Progress: across room to front door ✓ · turn left ✓ · up the stairs ✓ · turn right ✓ · enter bedroom ✓ · wait (STOP) ✓
Route: living room → foyer (front door) → winding staircase (several slide-offs on the curved bottom steps, solved by facing the flight squarely and climbing in short verified legs) → upper landing → right down the hall → around a mesh artifact → into the master bedroom
Steps used: 440/500 · tool calls: 63/80
```

The trickiest part was the curved staircase, which repeatedly deflected batched climbs back to the ground-floor plant corner; switching to short 4-6 step legs with visual correction at each winder got me up cleanly. Stopped ~2 steps inside the bedroom per "enter the bedroom then wait."
